macro_segments.title: Cases of Rape and Fine: When a Rapist Must Pay the Ketubah Fine Despite Deserving Death

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Mishnah on Cases Involving a Fine (Knas) Despite Deserving Death
Why the Rapist Is Not Executed Due to the Principle of Kam Lei (He Forfeits Money)
Gemara's Problem: If He Deserves Death (Chayav Karet), Why Does He Pay a Fine?
Solution: Once Penetration Begins (Ha'arah), She Becomes Pagum and Cannot Be Killed
Debate: When Does Pagam Occur and When Can Execution No Longer Happen?
Case Solutions: Anal Intercourse First, Vaginal Later; Woman Refusing Execution; Seduction Not Rape
Abbaye's Answer: Intervention Possible Without Lethal Force (Bahad Mei-Evarav)

macro_segments.title: The Pursuer (Rodeph): When Self-Defense Permits Killing and When It Does Not

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yonatan Ben Shaul: If a Pursuer Can Be Stopped Without Lethal Force, Killing Him Is Murder
Proof from the Woman's Miscarriage: A Case Where Perpetrator Must Pay Despite Being a Pursuer
The Gemara's Counterargument: Death to One, Payment to Another (Different Victims)
Rava's Teaching: A Pursuer Exempt Even for Third-Party Damage; Pursued Must Pay
Good Samaritan Principle: Intervener Exempt from Payment to Encourage Rescue

macro_segments.title: Lethal Intervention Against Grave Transgressions: Avodah Zarah and Shabbat Violations

micro_segments.title: Pursuer vs. Animal: No Intervention by Lethal Force When No Human Victim Exists
Rabbi Shimon Ben Yochai: Lethal Force Permitted to Prevent Avodah Zarah (via Kal Vachomer)
Rabbi Elazar BeRabbi Shimon: Shabbat Violation Also Permits Lethal Intervention via Gezeirah Shavah

macro_segments.title: Yehareg Ve-Al Ya'avor: When One Must Die Rather Than Transgress

micro_segments.title: The Three Categories: Avodah Zarah, Giluy Arayot, and Shfichut Damim—When Death Is Required
Avodah Zarah Debate: Rabbi Yishmael Permits It in Private; Rabbi Eliezer Requires Martyrdom
Rabbi Eliezer's Reasoning: Ahavat Hashem Demands Fidelity Even in Private Acts
Giluy Arayot: Same Reasoning as Murder—Death Preferred to Transgression
Murder: Why One Must Die Rather Than Kill—No One's Blood Is Redder Than Another's

macro_segments.title: Public Transgression and Gezerot Malchut: Limits on When Martyrdom Is Required

micro_segments.title: Rav Dimi: Except During Gezerot Malchut (Religious Persecution), One May Transgress in Private
Ravin's Stricter View: Even in Private, One Must Die for Transgression in Public (Befarhesya)
Defining Parhesya (Public): Ten Jews Required; Not About Physical Location but Community Impact
Exception for Self-Interest: If Perpetrator Acts for Own Benefit, Not to Force Transgression
The Esther Case: Woman as Karka Olam (Passive)—Different Halachic Status in Intercourse
